englishLines: And in mercy wash him clean of that defilement.
In order that the pardoning grace of God may be seen,
persianLines: غسل داده رحمت او را زین خباط
تا که غفاری او ظاهر شود
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Beholds by the light of God all things that exist.
His pure light, without signs or tokens,
persianLines: او به نور حق ببیند آنچ هست
نور پاکش بی‌دلیل و بی‌بیان
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: For I have fallen into this dire disgrace;
I have squandered goods and gold and wealth.
persianLines: کاندرین ادبار زشت افتاده‌ام
مال و زر و نعمت از کف داده‌ام
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: You will be able to build thousands of houses.
The treasure lies under it; there is no help for it;
persianLines: صد هزاران خانه شاید ساختن
گنج زیر خانه است و چاره نیست
disclosures: Abridged translation. Whinfield renders this section freely, omitting the extended cobbler's-shop couplets in the middle of the Persian and closing with two lines citing Sanai that fall outside this section. Line numbering does not correspond one-to-one with the Persian.

englishLines: Behold the bright and fresh sprays of coral,
And the princely fruits growing in the water of life!
persianLines: شاخهای تازه‌ی مرجان ببین
میوه‌های رسته ز آب جان ببین
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Nay, borrow from Him both eyes and sight,
And with those eyes of His look upon His face,
persianLines: بلک ازو کن عاریت چشم و نظر
پس ز چشم او بروی او نگر
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: "Acquiescence in infidelity is infidelity"
distinguish the ordinance from the ordained
persianLines: الرضا بالکفر کفر
پس قضا را خواجه از مقضی بدان
disclosures: This English segment is a composite. Its first half is a close, line-by-line rendering of the Persian section; its second half (the barber and Zaid anecdotes) belongs to a different Persian section and is not covered by this pairing.

englishLines: house of 'Isa was the banquet of men of heart
the Seven Sleepers' dog
persianLines: صومعه‌ی عیسیست خوان اهل دل
سگ کهف
disclosures: Abridged translation. The bulk of this passage corresponds to the Persian section on the afflicted gathering at Jesus's cloister; Whinfield condenses it and omits the Noah's-ark passage. The closing verses on false claims to sanctity are drawn from a different section of the Persian text.

englishLines: My hand is a tabor and my belly a drum,
Like the rose I beat the drum of love of water.
persianLines: دست چون دف و شکم همچون دهل
طبل عشق آب می‌کوبم چو گل
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: falcon lost his way ... inhabited by owls
light of the eye is mated with fat
persianLines: باز در ویران بر جغدان فتاد
تاب نور چشم با پیهست جفت
disclosures: This English passage is E. H. Whinfield's abridged Victorian translation. It renders a contiguous excerpt (lines 260-283) from a much longer Persian section; the surrounding story is compressed into English prose elsewhere in the block, and one or two English couplets have no counterpart in the Nicholson-aligned Persian text.

englishLines: Open clear eyes and look upon me,
That you may behold the light of God in a mortal.
persianLines: چشم نیکو باز کن در من نگر
تا ببینی نور حق اندر بشر
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: A demon, though he usurp his kingdom, is quite another.
The demon has taken upon him the form of Solomon,
persianLines: دیو گرچه ملک گیرد هست غیر
دیو بر شبه سلیمان کرد ایست
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Worldly senses are the ladder of earth, / Spiritual senses ... of heaven
Has cut its flesh to extract a spear-head, / Causing a fresh skin to grow
persianLines: حس دنیا نردبان این جهان / حس دینی نردبان آسمان
پوست را بشکافت و پیکان را کشید / پوست تازه ... بر دمید
disclosures: This English passage is E. H. Whinfield's abridged translation. It renders only the closing discourse of the Persian section (from "Worldly senses are the ladder of earth" onward), omitting the verse narrative of the parrot — summarised separately as prose — and a substantial intervening passage of the discourse. The subheading "Confusion of saints with hypocrites" is the translator's addition and does not appear in the Persian.

englishLines: He added, "'Yea, he was with you,' 9 this great king;
He beheld our actions and heard our secrets.
persianLines: گفت و هو معکم این شاه بود
فعل ما می‌دید و سرمان می‌شنود
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: snake-catcher ... frozen snake ... took it to Baghdad
Lust is that snake; How say you it is dead? It is only frozen
persianLines: حکایت مارگیر کی اژدهای فسرده را مرده پنداشت ... آورد به بغداد
نفست اژدرهاست او کی مرده است ... افسرده است
disclosures: Whinfield's abridgement summarises most of this section in his own English prose and renders only its closing verse passage. The paired verse corresponds closely to the Persian; the preceding narrative summary is the translator's prose, not translated verse.

englishLines: And when they enjoy prosperity they do evil.
Wherefore the mosque of their devotion is hell,
persianLines: چون وفا بینند خود جافی شوند
مسجد طاعاتشان پس دوزخست
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Even till the time of Pharaoh and gentle Moses.
Between these two the war was waged for years,
persianLines: تا به فرعون و به موسی شفیق
سالها اندر میانشان حرب بود
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: From that breath of the soul roses bloomed in his heart,
His soul heard the revelations of heaven;
persianLines: زان دم جان در دل او گل شکفت
جان او بشنید وحی آسمان
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Within you is a Nimrod ... first become an Abraham
saint handles earth, it becomes gold; sinner handles gold, it turns to dust
persianLines: در تو نمرودیست آتش در مرو / رفت خواهی اول ابراهیم شو
کاملی گر خاک گیرد زر شود / ناقص ار زر برد خاکستر شود
disclosures: This English passage corresponds line-for-line to the Persian section, but appears under a translator-supplied subheading; the Nicholson edition places these verses under a narrative heading about the merchant and the parrots of Hindustan. One couplet is translated freely, substituting a sea-and-pearls image for the Persian rose-from-fire.

englishLines: Know to be shadows of the architect's thought;
Although stones and planks and bricks
persianLines: سایه‌ی اندیشه‌ی معمار دان
گرچه خود اندر محل افتکار
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Without the decree and command of that Lord of lords;
Not a morsel goes from the mouth down the throat
persianLines: بی قضا و حکم آن سلطان بخت
از دهان لقمه نشد سوی گلو
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: O Commander of the faithful
so noble a bird be confined in a cage
persianLines: کای امیرالممنین
مرغ بی‌اندازه چون شد در قفص
disclosures: The translated verse corresponds closely, couplet by couplet and in the same order, to Nicholson section 633. Two differences: the block is preceded by the translator's own English prose summary of the surrounding frame story, which is not translated verse; and the final few couplets of the English continue past the end of this Persian section into adjoining material.

englishLines: He has thousands of names, yet is One,
Answering to all of His descriptions, yet indescribable.
persianLines: صد هزاران نام و او یک آدمی
صاحب هر وصفش از وصفی عمی
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: The Old Man and the Physician
Fools laud and magnify the mosque, while they strive to oppress holy men of heart
persianLines: شکایت گفتن پیرمردی به طبیب
ابلهان تعظیم مسجد می‌کنند / در جفای اهل دل جد می‌کنند
disclosures: Abridged translation. E. H. Whinfield renders the closing verses of this section but omits roughly fourteen intervening couplets, and one line (the proverb about the urn and the well) has no counterpart in the Persian. The opening sentence is the translator's own summary heading rather than translated verse.

englishLines: You must have the soul of Abraham in order with light
To see the mansions of Paradise in the fire.
persianLines: جان ابراهیم باید تا به نور
بیند اندر نار فردوس و قصور
disclosures: Whinfield's translation is abridged and freely amplified here. He omits sixteen consecutive couplets of the Persian (a digression on deficient knowledge and lightning) and expands Rumi's six-couplet "through love" catalogue to eleven couplets, adding image-pairs (thorns/roses, stake/throne, devil/houri, lions/mice and others) that do not appear in the Nicholson-aligned Persian text. The English block's opening prose is Whinfield's own summary, not translated verse, and its heading corresponds to a different Persian section from the verse translated.

englishLines: I conceal myself a while from this world,
I shake down the leaves of outward sense from the tree.
persianLines: زین جهان خود را دمی پنهان کنم
برگ حس را از درخت افشان کنم
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: insufficient for a camel / ocean to a mouse
four loaves ... voracious as a duck
persianLines: آب جو نسبت باشتر هست کم / لیک باشد موش را آن همچو یم
وظیفه چار نان ... اسیر حرص مانند بط است
disclosures: This English text is E. H. Whinfield's abridged Victorian translation. It condenses the Persian, omitting several passages, and draws on two Nicholson sections rather than one: the bulk corresponds to "عذر گفتن فقیر به شیخ", while the closing lines correspond to the separate section "بیان دعویی که عین آن دعوی گواه صدق خویش است". Line-by-line correspondence with the Persian should not be assumed.

englishLines: The second, he, namely, who is half wise,
Knows the wise man to be the light of his eyes.
persianLines: دیگری که نیم‌عاقل آمد او
عاقلی را دیده‌ی خود داند او
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Though the real nature of Noah is not revealed to you.
On the other hand, if you say, "What know I of Noah
persianLines: گرچه ماهیت نشد از نوح کشف
ور بگویی من چه دانم نوح را
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: The eye of passion takes milk to be blood.
Kiss that arrow and bear it to the King,
persianLines: چشم خشمت خون شمارد شیر را
بوسه ده بر تیر و پیش شاه بر
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: A certain man was going into the mosque,
Just as another was coming out.
persianLines: آن یکی می‌رفت در مسجد درون
مردم از مسجد همی‌آمد برون
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: The soul of David was warmed by its heat,
Iron became pliable by the force of his weaving. 4
persianLines: جان داوود از شعاعش گرم شد
آهن اندر دست‌بافش نرم شد
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: knocked at the door of his friend
"who art thou. O faithful one?"
persianLines: آن یکی آمد در یاری بزد
کیستی ای معتمد
disclosures: Whinfield's Victorian translation is abridged: it renders the opening couplets of this Persian section and stops at "no room for two 'I's in one house," omitting the extended passage that follows in the original.

englishLines: In Solomon we hold our course to eternity.
Along with Solomon plunge into the ocean, 4
persianLines: در سلیمان تا ابد داریم سیر
با سلیمان پای در دریا بنه
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: He said, "If I tarry in one house two days,
Attachment to that house becomes a passion with me.
persianLines: گفت در یک خانه گر باشم دو روز
عشق آن مسکن کند در من فروز
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Yea, he dwells in security forever.
His form is vanished, he is a mere mirror;
persianLines: تا ابد در آمنی او ساکنست
نقش او فانی و او شد آینه
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: The water is become muddy; close the mouth of the well,
Till God makes it again pure and sweet;
persianLines: آب تیره شد سر چه بند کن
تا خدایش باز صاف و خوش کند
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Like you, I was naturally dark,
'Twas the Sun's revelation that gave me such light.
persianLines: چون شما تاریک بودم در نهاد
وحی خورشیدم چنین نوری بداد
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Thou art as joy, and we are laughing;
The laughter is the consequence of the joy.
persianLines: تو مثال شادی و ما خنده‌ایم
که نتیجه‌ی شادی فرخنده‌ایم
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: He drew forth a mirror from his side
The tinder is the mirror of the flint and steel
persianLines: آینه بیرون کشید او از بغل
سوخته هم آینه‌ی آتش‌زنه‌ست
disclosures: This English text is E. H. Whinfield's abridged translation. It renders a continuous run of verses from the middle of the Persian section: the section's opening lines are condensed into Whinfield's prose summary rather than translated, and the closing third of the Persian section is omitted.

englishLines: Does a cow ever seek the lion?
Did the wolf show love to Joseph, 5
persianLines: گاو سوی شیر نر کی رو نهد
گرگ بر یوسف کجا عشق آورد
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: He is like Pharaoh, and his body is like Moses,
He runs abroad crying, "where is my foe?"
persianLines: خود حسود و دشمن او آن تنست
او چو فرعون و تنش موسی او
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Or like ice which is formed in great frost,
The frost departs, but the ice is still before us.
persianLines: یا مثال یخ که زاید از شتا
شد شتا پنهان و آن یخ پیش ما
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: He has the scent of the apple, but not a piece of it,
And the scent only for the purpose of misleading others.
persianLines: بوی سیبش هست جزو سیب نیست
بو درو جز از پی آسیب نیست
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Is a secret denier and philosopher.
Now and then he displays firm belief,
persianLines: در جهان او فلسفی پنهانیست
می‌نماید اعتقاد و گاه گاه
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Then he inquires what the laughter was about,
And, on hearing it, proceeds to laugh a second time.
persianLines: باز وا پرسد که خنده بر چه بود
پس دوم کرت بخندد چون شنود
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: See the kings who lead forth their armies
To slay their own people from envy!
persianLines: پادشاهان بین که لشکر می‌کشند
از حسد خویشان خود را می‌کشند
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: Which is distinct from the light of reason and sense.
At night there is no light, and colors are not seen;
persianLines: کو ز نور عقل و حس پاک و جداست
شب نبد نور و ندیدی رنگها
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: In difficulties there is provided a way of salvation
that eagle of the Prophet's / Which carried off the sandal
persianLines: ان مع العسر یسرا
آن عقابش را عقابی دان که او / در ربود آن موزه

englishLines: God said, "Do thou grant his earnest request... according to his freewill"
Freewill is as the salt to piety
persianLines: گفت یزدان تو بده بایست او / برگشا در اختیار آن دست او
اختیار آمد عبادت را نمک
disclosures: Abridged translation. E. H. Whinfield renders this section closely but not completely: the English omits the section's closing couplets, and substitutes "wasps" for the Persian "snake" in the honeybee/serpent contrast. The block also opens with Whinfield's own prose summary of the surrounding story, which is not translated verse.

englishLines: And, to please God, have quenched those fires,
So that the fire of lust, that erst breathed flame,
persianLines: نار را کشتید از بهر خدا
آتش شهوت که شعله می‌زدی
disclosures: Whinfield is an abridged edition. This record publishes one continuous two-line English excerpt aligned to one continuous Nicholson couplet; the wider verse segment may omit surrounding Persian material.

englishLines: The Prophet said to 'Ali, "O 'Ali, Thou art the Lion of God"
His shadow on earth is as that of Mount Qaf
persianLines: گفت پیغامبر علی را کای علی / شیر حقی
ظل او اندر زمین چون کوه قاف
disclosures: English text is E. H. Whinfield's abridged Victorian translation. It follows this Persian section couplet-for-couplet and in order, but includes several additional couplets that fall outside the bounds of this section. The tattoo story named in the English heading is narrated separately as prose and belongs to a different Persian section.
